_about: v0.2.8 — legacy-only sample. No top-level structured source_resolution, only the v0.2.5-style intake_quality.source_resolution list. The utility must upgrade it on the fly to a structured summary, and the renderer / audit / quality gate must not crash.

source_resolution: Input string: 'How to Read a Paper'.
Resolver status: matched; match_type: title; confidence: high.
Matched resolver hint: 'How to Read a Paper' → How to Read a Paper (S. Keshav, 2007).
Resolver step: cli overlay via p3pr paper_title subcommand; input='How to Read a Paper'

summaries.one_sentence: Keshav's three-pass method turns a paper into something you can read, summarize, and reconstruct in three passes.
three_sentence: Pass 1 is a five-minute bird's-eye view: read the title, abstract, intro, section headings, conclusion; jot the Five Cs.
Pass 2 reads the paper but skips the math: aim to understand 90% of the content, write a one-page summary, and identify the open questions.
Pass 3 re-derives the paper from scratch, then compares your reconstruction to the authors' claims to find hidden assumptions and limitations.
ten_sentence: Three-pass reading is a deliberate division of labour between surveying, understanding, and reconstructing.
Pass 1 should take about 5-10 minutes and produces a one-paragraph 'gist' of the paper.
The Five Cs are: Category, Context, Correctness, Contributions, Clarity.
Pass 1 also produces a first guess at the 'read?' decision.
Pass 2 reads the body of the paper but does not yet verify the math.
Pass 2 should be able to write a one-page summary that captures the key claims.
Pass 3 verifies the math, re-derives the main result, and reads the related work.
Pass 3 surfaces hidden assumptions, edge cases, and limitations.
Three-pass reading is most useful for unfamiliar research areas; in your own area one pass is enough.
The method scales from conference papers to theses.

five_cs.context: Before this paper, students had no widely taught, systematic way to read research papers.
five_cs.correctness: The three-pass method is well supported by decades of teaching experience and is widely adopted.
contributions: First formalization of the three-pass reading method.
A practical Five Cs checklist for Pass 1.
five_cs.clarity: Short, well-structured, easy to teach in one lecture.

pass1.context: Students entering research need a fast way to triage papers.
pass1.decision: Worth reading once and keeping as a reference.

main_ideas: Pass 2 = read body, skip math, write one-page summary.
pass2.method_summary: Three-pass method with the Five Cs (Category, Context, Correctness, Contributions, Clarity) used in Pass 1.

method_reconstruction: Pass 1: read title, abstract, intro, headings, conclusion in 5-10 min.
Pass 2: read body, skip math, write summary.
Pass 3: re-derive result, find hidden assumptions.
critical_review: The 5-minute budget is generous for short papers but tight for theses.
Pass 3 is hard to do well without domain knowledge.

claims_evidence_map.claim: Three-pass reading is faster than one-pass reading for unfamiliar papers.
claims_evidence_map.label: [Author claim]

glossary.definition: One of three reading stages in Keshav's method.

limitations: 5-minute budget may be too short for very long papers.

steps: Pick 3 unfamiliar papers in a new field.
Write the 1-page summary at the end of pass 2.
success_criteria: You can state the 3 main claims of each paper after Pass 2.

open_questions: How does the 3-pass method change for review papers vs. research papers?
Is there a 4-pass variant for theses?
final_checklist: I can describe the three-pass method.
I can list the Five Cs.
I can write a 1-page summary of an unfamiliar paper in 1 hour.
